chapter_summary: Krishna performs the Rasa dance with the gopis in Vrindavan, expanding himself to dance with each gopi simultaneously. The chapter describes the ecstatic pastime, the reactions of celestial beings, and concludes with a philosophical justification by Shuka for Krishna's seemingly transgressive behavior, emphasizing his divine nature and the purpose of such pastimes for the liberation of devotees.

story.title: The Rasa Dance of Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's divine pastimes in Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's youth in Vrindavan, after the previous chapter's events.
story.comic_potential: The playful and ecstatic dance, with Krishna expanding himself, has humorous and wondrous elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are beyond moral judgment; his pastimes are for the benefit of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that the divine play (lila) is a means to transcend conventional morality and realize the absolute nature of the self, which is beyond duality.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and narrating Krishna's pastimes with faith leads to devotion and liberation from material desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views this as a practice of shravana (hearing) and kirtana (chanting), which are key components of the path, purifying the mind and awakening divine love.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama rescue Nanda from a serpent, then later kill the demon Shankhachuda and retrieve his jewel. The serpent is revealed to be a cursed vidyadhara named Sudarshana, who is liberated by Krishna's touch.

story.title: The Rescue of Nanda and the Killing of Shankhachuda
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's childhood in Vrindavan, after the incident of the serpent Kaliya.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic rescue of Nanda from the serpent, the liberation of the vidyadhara, and the chase and killing of Shankhachuda with the gopis watching.

teachings.teaching: The touch of the Lord's feet purifies even the most sinful and liberates them from curses.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes that direct contact with the divine (through devotion or grace) instantly removes karmic impurities and grants liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and responds to their call for help.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that sincere prayer (prapanna) activates the divine response, as seen when Nanda calls out and Krishna rescues him.

teachings.teaching: Pride in one's beauty or status leads to downfall, but a curse from sages can be a blessing in disguise if it leads to the Lord's contact.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that the vidyadhara's pride was a karmic obstacle; the curse was a grace that brought him to Krishna's feet, illustrating that adversity can be a spiritual opportunity.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's name purifies both the speaker and the listener.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights the power of nama-sankirtana; even uttering Krishna's name cleanses all, as stated by Sudarshana.

chapter_summary: The gopīs describe Kṛṣṇa's enchanting flute-playing and his pastimes in Vṛndāvana, which captivate all beings—animals, rivers, trees, celestial maidens, and even the gods. They express their own intense longing and devotion, highlighting Kṛṣṇa's divine nature as the Supreme Lord.

story.title: The Gopīs' Song of Kṛṣṇa's Flute
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Vṛndāvana pastimes
story.timeline_note: During Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vṛndāvana, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The gopīs' exaggerated descriptions of nature's response to Kṛṣṇa's flute, such as rivers stopping and animals becoming statues, have a whimsical, poetic quality.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine sound (flute) to captivate and purify all beings, leading them to spiritual absorption.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the flute represents the inner sound (nada) that draws the mind inward, stilling the senses and merging with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Devotion (bhakti) expressed through constant remembrance and singing of the Lord's pastimes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kīrtana (chanting) is a direct method to still the mind and awaken the heart, as seen in the gopīs' absorption.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is available to all, regardless of external purity, as seen in the rivers, trees, and animals responding.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that divine grace is impartial and accessible through sincere longing.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord leads to freedom from worldly attachments.
teachings.lens_note: The gopīs' abandonment of household duties mirrors the Kriya-Yoga ideal of inner renunciation while performing actions.

chapter_summary: Ariṣṭa, a demon in the form of a bull, terrorizes Vraja. Kṛṣṇa kills him, and Nārada informs Kaṃsa that Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma are the sons of Vasudeva and Devakī, destined to kill him. Kaṃsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devakī, and sends Keśī to kill the brothers. He also plots to have them killed by the elephant Kuvalayāpīḍa and wrestlers during a bow sacrifice, and orders Akrūra to bring them to Mathurā.

story.title: The Killing of Ariṣṭa and Kaṃsa's Plot
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's childhood pastimes in Vraja and the events leading to the Mathurā episode
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vraja, before the Mathurā pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic battle with a bull demon, divine revelations, and a villain's scheming.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine will (Kṛṣṇa) eliminates negative tendencies (demons) to protect the spiritual aspirant's progress.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to the results of actions; success and failure depend on divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment from outcomes, focusing on the action itself as a service to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Even a king's plans are subject to divine providence.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle that the ego's plans are futile without alignment with the higher Self.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Kesi in the form of a horse, then later kills the demon Vyoma (son of Maya) who had kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Narada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future exploits.

story.title: Killing of Kesi and Vyoma
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: Occurs before Krishna leaves for Mathura; Narada's prophecy refers to future events including the killing of Kaṃsa and others.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic fight with a giant horse demon, playful kidnapping of cowherd boys, and a cave rescue.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme self, the witness, the controller of all, who creates and destroys the universe through his maya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the inner self (atman) is identical to Krishna, and the practice of yoga reveals this unity beyond the play of maya.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that this protection is not external but internal: through yoga, one destroys inner demons (vices) and protects the sattvic qualities.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's actions are effortless and playful (lila), even in killing demons.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of acting without attachment, performing all actions as a divine play.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, meditating on Kṛṣṇa's glories and his own good fortune. Upon arrival, he sees the footprints of Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, then meets them personally. He falls at their feet, is embraced, and receives hospitality from Nanda and the brothers.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra, sent by Kaṃsa, travels to Gokula. On the way, he contemplates Kṛṣṇa's divine nature and his own fortune. He arrives, sees Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, falls at their feet, and is warmly received. Nanda inquires about their welfare under Kaṃsa's rule.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's childhood pastimes in Vraja, before Kaṃsa's death.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of ecstatic devotion while traveling, contrasting with his official mission as Kaṃsa's spy.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is extremely rare and is the result of great merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the culmination of sustained practice (abhyāsa) and detachment (vairāgya), leading to direct perception of the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's feet are the refuge for those afraid of death (time).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that surrender to the Lord (prapatti) is the ultimate Kriya-Yoga, dissolving the ego and fear of mortality.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial, yet He reciprocates with His devotees according to their devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of 'yathā bhāva tathā siddhi' – as one's feeling, so the result. In Kriya-Yoga, the quality of inner attitude determines spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to give up pretense, fear, and sorrow by seeing, hearing, and contemplating the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses constant remembrance (smaraṇa) and association with the divine as the means to purify the mind and attain liberation.

chapter_summary: Akrūra arrives in Vraja to escort Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma to Mathurā for the festival. The gopīs lament the impending separation, expressing their devotion and anguish. Kṛṣṇa consoles them and departs. On the way, at the Yamunā, Akrūra performs ablutions and has a vision of the cosmic form of the Lord, including Śeṣa and the four-armed Viṣṇu.

story.title: Akrūra's Arrival and the Gopīs' Lament
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's departure from Vraja to Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Pūtanā and other demons, before the Mathurā festival.
story.comic_potential: High: The gopīs' dramatic lament, Akrūra's underwater vision, and the emotional farewell.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's devotees, like the gopīs, experience intense separation (viraha) which deepens their love.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this viraha is the intense longing that purifies the heart and leads to the highest state of divine union.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme object of love; nothing else is ultimately desirable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Kriya-Yoga practitioner must cultivate single-pointed devotion, renouncing all other attachments.

teachings.teaching: The divine vision (darśana) of the Lord's cosmic form is granted to the pure-hearted devotee.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the inner vision of the Self (ātman) achieved through meditation and grace.

chapter_summary: Akrura, filled with devotion, sees Krishna in his divine form adorned with Srivatsa, Kaustubha, and a garland, surrounded by associates like Sunanda, Nanda, Sanaka, and sages including Prahlada and Narada. He praises Krishna with a choked voice, bowing down with folded hands.

story.title: Akrura's Vision and Praise of Krishna
story.arc: Krishna's Vrindavan and Mathura pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return to Mathura, before the slaying of Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's overwhelmed devotion and the divine vision of Krishna surrounded by deities and sages.

teachings.teaching: The devotee sees the Lord's divine form with intense love and devotion, leading to ecstatic symptoms like hair standing on end and tears.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the culmination of bhakti-yoga where the practitioner's prana and consciousness merge with the divine, causing spontaneous physical reactions as the kundalini rises.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is served by his own potencies (Sri, Pusti, etc.) and worshipped by great sages and devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of 'svarupa-sakti' in Kriya-Yoga: the divine energies are not separate but manifestations of the Lord's own being, and the devotee's service aligns with these energies.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Krishna, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the primordial Puruṣa. He describes how all elements, senses, and deities emanate from Him, yet they cannot comprehend His true nature. Akrūra explains various paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, jñāna, and devotion—all ultimately leading to Krishna. He then enumerates Krishna's cosmic form and His avatars (Matsya, Hayagrīva, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Buddha, Kalki). Akrūra confesses his own ignorance and delusion caused by māyā, and surrenders at Krishna's feet, seeking protection.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Krishna
story.arc: Akrūra, having arrived in Vraja, offers a profound hymn of surrender and praise to Krishna, acknowledging His supremacy and seeking refuge.
story.timeline_note: This occurs during Krishna's earthly pastimes, after Akrūra has come to take Krishna and Balarāma to Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: The chapter is a prayer, not a narrative, so comic potential is low. However, the vivid descriptions of avatars could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the ultimate cause of all causes, the source of Brahmā and the universe.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Supreme Self as the inner witness and the source of all prāṇa and consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The elements, senses, and deities are manifestations of Krishna's cosmic form.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the microcosmic correspondence: the body's elements and senses are expressions of the divine, to be purified through kriya.

teachings.teaching: All paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, jñāna, and devotion—ultimately lead to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga synthesizes these paths: the inner sacrifice of prāṇāyāma, the yoga of uniting breath and mind, and the jñāna of self-awareness all culminate in devotion to the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is deluded by māyā, identifying with body and possessions, and suffers in saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga diagnoses this as the root cause of bondage: ignorance of one's true nature as ātman, which is removed by the practice of kriya-yoga and surrender.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to Krishna is the only means to overcome the restless mind and attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender is not passive but active: through disciplined practice, the mind is brought under control and offered to the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrūra witnesses the divine form of Kṛṣṇa in the Yamunā, then returns to Mathurā with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and Kṛṣṇa kills a washerman who refuses to give garments, then blesses a garland-maker named Sudāmā.

story.title: Akrūra's Vision and Entry into Mathurā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the vision in the Yamunā, before the killing of Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's beheading with a hand gesture and the women's frantic rush to see Kṛṣṇa are vivid.

teachings.teaching: The Lord reveals his divine form to his devotee in a flash, then withdraws it.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the momentary glimpse of the Self (ātman) during deep meditation, which then dissolves back into the unmanifest.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the cause of the universe and appears for the welfare of the world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the descent (avatāra) of the divine is to restore dharma and guide practitioners on the path of kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is equal to all beings, but shows special favor to his devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle that while the Self is impartial, the grace flows more readily to those who turn inward with devotion.

teachings.teaching: True wealth is devotion to the Lord, not material possessions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret Sudāmā's request for bhakti as the highest goal, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga aim of unbroken union with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Krishna's boon to Sudama, where Sudama returns home and finds his hut transformed into a palace. Krishna's blessings include friendship with devotees, compassion for all beings, and prosperity for his family. The chapter concludes with Krishna and Balarama leaving the scene.

story.title: Krishna's Blessings to Sudama
story.arc: Sudama's transformation from poverty to prosperity through devotion
story.timeline_note: After Sudama's visit to Dwaraka, Krishna bestows the boon and departs.
story.comic_potential: Sudama's humble hut turning into a palace could be depicted with a magical transformation sequence.

teachings.teaching: Friendship with devotees and compassion for all beings are key virtues.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes cultivating bhakti (devotion) and karuna (compassion) as essential practices for spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna brings material and spiritual prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework interprets this as the result of sincere kriya-yoga practice, where inner purification leads to outer abundance.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchback Trivakra, straighten her, break the great bow, kill the guards, and prepare for the wrestling match arranged by Kamsa.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to Kamsa's death
story.timeline_note: After leaving Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura on the day of the bow festival.
story.comic_potential: The straightening of Trivakra and the breaking of the bow are visually striking and humorous.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch can transform and elevate even the most lowly and crooked.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes how the practice of yoga straightens the kundalini and purifies the nadis, leading to spiritual transformation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge for those on the path.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the guru (Krishna) is the shelter for the practitioner, just as Krishna promised to visit Trivakra's home.

teachings.teaching: Material power is fragile and can be broken by divine will.
teachings.lens_note: The breaking of the bow represents the dissolution of ego and material attachments through the power of yoga.

teachings.teaching: Fear of death arises from ignorance; the wise see the Lord's hand in all events.
teachings.lens_note: Kamsa's omens and fear illustrate the state of one who is attached to the body; yoga frees one from such fear.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter the wrestling arena after killing the elephant Kuvalayapida. They are praised by the spectators, who recount Krishna's past deeds. The wrestler Chanura challenges them to a match, and Krishna accepts, emphasizing fairness.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the killing of the elephant, before the wrestling match with Chanura and Mushtika.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic elephant fight, crowd reactions, and the buildup to the wrestling match.

teachings.teaching: One should act to please the king, as subjects find welfare by pleasing the ruler.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning one's actions with the divine will (Ishvara pranidhana), seeing the king as a representative of cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: The king is the embodiment of all beings; pleasing him pleases all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes seeing the divine in all beings; the king as a symbol of the universal self.

teachings.teaching: Wrestling should be fair, with matched opponents, to avoid adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga stresses righteous action (dharma) in all endeavors; fairness in competition mirrors inner discipline.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama engage in a wrestling match with Kamsa's wrestlers, killing Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala. Krishna then leaps onto Kamsa's dais, drags him down, and kills him. Balarama kills Kamsa's brothers. The women of Kamsa's court lament. Krishna and Balarama release their parents from bondage and bow to them.

story.title: The Killing of Kamsa
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura
story.timeline_note: After the wrestling match, Krishna kills Kamsa and releases his parents.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic wrestling, divine feats, and a villain's comeuppance.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine will eliminates obstacles (wicked tendencies) to reveal the true self.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with those who support adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the mind must avoid environments that foster negative habits; the assembly that tolerates adharma is to be shunned.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge and controller of all.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the realization of the inner Self as the witness and controller of all mental modifications.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan with gifts. Krishna and Balarama then undergo their sacred thread ceremony, study under sage Sandipani in Avanti, and after mastering all arts, retrieve Sandipani's son from the ocean demon Panchajana and from Yama's city, thus fulfilling their guru-dakshina.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura as young boys, then undergo upanayana and study under Sandipani, completing their education in 64 days.
story.comic_potential: Krishna and Balarama as divine students mastering all subjects instantly, and the humorous scene of the ocean god offering gifts to the two boys.

teachings.teaching: One must serve and honor one's parents, as Krishna expresses regret for not being able to serve Vasudeva and Devaki due to Kamsa's persecution.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the importance of fulfilling one's duties (dharma) with devotion, even when external circumstances hinder. The emotional release of tears symbolizes purification of the heart.

teachings.teaching: A son who does not support his parents in old age is condemned to eat his own flesh in the next life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that neglecting one's parents is a form of spiritual suicide; the 'eating one's own flesh' symbolizes the karmic consequence of severing the life-giving bond.

teachings.teaching: The guru must be honored with appropriate dakshina; Krishna and Balarama go to great lengths to retrieve the guru's son from death.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of 'guru-seva' as a path to transcendence. The retrieval from Yama's realm symbolizes overcoming the cycle of death through devotion and surrender.

teachings.teaching: Even the divine Lord, when playing the role of a human, follows the norms of society, such as undergoing the sacred thread ceremony and studying under a guru.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a demonstration that spiritual practice (kriya-yoga) requires discipline and humility, even for the enlightened. The Lord's example validates the path of sadhana.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. He delivers Krishna's message, describes his divine nature, and assures them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna sends Uddhava to console his parents and the gopis in Vrindavan, who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message and philosophical teachings, assuring them of his return.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura and killing of Kamsa, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The emotional reunion and the gopis' morning activities provide rich visual and dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge for those who surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of complete surrender (prapatti) as a means to attain union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond material qualities and actions, yet He engages in pastimes for the protection of the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights that the Lord's actions are spontaneous and not bound by karma, serving as a model for detached action.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner Self of all beings, and one should see Him everywhere.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of seeing the divine in all, fostering universal love and compassion.

teachings.teaching: The mind absorbed in the Lord's pastimes becomes purified and attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the power of smarana (remembrance) as a key practice in Kriya-Yoga to still the mind and realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the cause of all causes, the source of both material and spiritual worlds.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the non-dual nature of reality, a core tenet in Sharma's commentary.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain deeply attached to Krishna.

story.title: The Gopis' Message to Krishna
story.arc: Uddhava's visit to Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura and killing of Kamsa, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic and emotional outbursts, their playful yet angry address to the bee, and Uddhava's attempts to console them offer rich comedic and poignant moments.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dream, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct realization of the self through meditation; this teaching aligns with the goal of discerning the witness consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna (or the divine) is the highest path, surpassing all other spiritual practices.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, continuous inner remembrance of the divine is a key practice, akin to the gopis' unwavering focus on Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Detachment from worldly attachments is necessary for spiritual progress; the gopis exemplify this by renouncing everything for Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework values vairagya (dispassion) as essential for Kriya-Yoga; the gopis' renunciation is a model.

teachings.teaching: The pain of separation from the divine can be a powerful catalyst for deepening devotion and spiritual growth.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga views longing for the divine as a transformative force; the gopis' viraha (separation) intensifies their love.

teachings.teaching: True happiness lies in freedom from desire (nairasya), as illustrated by the story of Pingala.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary often highlights the peace that comes from inner contentment, echoing Pingala's realization.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and others who are separated from Krishna. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised as the highest devotion. Uddhava then returns to Mathura and reports to Krishna, Balarama, and Vasudeva.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the residents.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's empathetic stay and the gopis' intense love create a poignant narrative.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even that of great yogis and sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this exemplifies the pinnacle of bhakti where the devotee's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of external purity; sincere practice yields results.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the feet of the gopis purifies the three worlds; their songs about Krishna are supremely purifying.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of association with pure devotees, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray that their mind, speech, and body be always engaged in Krishna's service.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of integrating all aspects of life into divine service, harmonizing thought, word, and deed.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, grants her a boon, then visits Akrura, who praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord. Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Courtesan
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful visit to the courtesan and Akrura's ecstatic praise.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is not bound by material qualities or actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching emphasizes the transcendence of the Self beyond the three gunas, a key insight for the practitioner to realize their own true nature as pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the purpose of yoga to restore inner balance and harmony.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or deities made of material elements.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The company of realized beings is a powerful aid in Kriya-Yoga practice, accelerating purification.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura to assess the situation of the Pāṇḍavas. He meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī expresses her sorrow and devotion to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and family tensions
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional plea, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; each individual must perform their own kriya and face their own karma.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired by adharma is taken by others; one who hoards it is abandoned by wealth, life, and family.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against attachment to material gains from improper means, which distracts from the yogic path.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream, illusion, and mental construct; one should control the mind and remain equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on inner stillness and detachment from the transient world through meditation.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of Īśvara, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as acknowledging the divine plan; the yogi surrenders to the cosmic will.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa is the supreme lord, incomprehensible in his play, and the cause of the cycle of saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the ultimate reality beyond intellectual grasp, realized through direct yogic experience.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates Jarāsandha's forces but spares him, knowing he will return. After seventeen defeats, Jarāsandha is captured by Balarāma but released by Kṛṣṇa for a future purpose. Later, the Yavana king Kālayavana, incited by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress city in the sea to protect the Yadus.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and establishment of Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā seventeen times; on the eighteenth, Kālayavana appears.
story.comic_potential: Kṛṣṇa's playful yet strategic battles, the dramatic escape to Dvārakā.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣā: the divine descends to restore balance when adharma prevails.

teachings.teaching: A true warrior does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vikalpa (vacillation); Kṛṣṇa's reply to Jarāsandha's taunt exemplifies decisive action.

teachings.teaching: Even in victory, one may spare an enemy for a higher purpose.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the yogic principle of viveka (discernment) — not all battles need be fought to the finish; sometimes restraint serves a greater plan.

chapter_summary: Krishna establishes his capital Dvaraka, receiving gifts from the gods including the assembly hall Sudharma and the Parijata tree. He then goes out unarmed to meet the approaching army.

story.title: Establishment of Dvaraka and Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna's early reign in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's victory over Jarasandha and establishment of Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: The scene of gods personally delivering gifts to Krishna has visual grandeur.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma, Parijata) symbolize that a devotee of the Lord receives all opulences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that when one is established in the self (Krishna), the universe offers its treasures without effort.

teachings.teaching: Krishna goes out unarmed, trusting in divine protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes non-attachment and reliance on inner strength; Krishna's action exemplifies surrender to the divine will.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king of the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been granted a boon by the gods to sleep in a cave after a long war. When the Yavana king pursued Krishna into the cave, Mucukunda awoke and incinerated the Yavana with his angry glance. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers. Krishna granted Mucukunda a boon, and Mucukunda chose liberation through devotion.

story.title: Mucukunda's Deliverance
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's killing of Kamsa and other demons, before the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana's futile chase and Mucukunda's sleepy anger turning him to ash is visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are countless and cannot be enumerated.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the infinite nature of divine grace and the futility of intellectual grasping.

teachings.teaching: Human life is rare and should be used for devotion, not worldly attachments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that the body is a temporary vessel; one should practice inner yoga rather than identify with material roles.

teachings.teaching: Time (Kala) is the supreme power, a manifestation of the Lord, and destroys all.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, time is the force that drives karmic cycles; awareness of time's power inspires urgent spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, recounts his past lives as a powerful king who conquered the world but remained unsatisfied. He realizes the futility of material pursuits and expresses deep desire for liberation and devotion to Kṛṣṇa. Kṛṣṇa praises his unwavering devotion and grants him boons: unalloyed bhakti, purification from sins, and in the next life he will become a great sage and attain Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation and Boon
story.arc: Mucukunda, after a long sleep, meets Kṛṣṇa and expresses his disillusionment with material life. He prays for pure devotion and liberation. Kṛṣṇa praises his detachment and grants him unalloyed bhakti, purification, and a future birth as a sage.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Kālayavana, Kṛṣṇa meets Mucukunda in a cave.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's dramatic recounting of his past lives and his humble plea for liberation, contrasted with Kṛṣṇa's compassionate response.

teachings.teaching: Material achievements and sense pleasures ultimately lead to dissatisfaction and bondage; true happiness lies in devotion to the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the need to transcend the guṇas through disciplined practice and surrender to the inner Self, which alone brings lasting peace.

teachings.teaching: Association with saintly persons (sat-saṅga) is the cause of awakening devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Sat-saṅga corresponds to the company of those established in Kriya-Yoga, which accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: One should not desire even liberation if it is separate from serving the Lord's lotus feet; pure devotion is the highest goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of offering all actions to the Divine without personal motive, leading to egolessness.

teachings.teaching: The mind of non-devotees, even if controlled by prāṇāyāma, remains impure due to unfulfilled desires; only devotion makes the mind steady.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga techniques must be coupled with devotion; otherwise, they are insufficient to uproot vāsanās.

teachings.teaching: One can engage in worldly duties while keeping the mind fixed on the Lord; such actions do not bind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the essence of Kriya-Yoga karma-yoga: performing actions without attachment, dedicating them to the Divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the events leading to Krishna's marriage with Rukmini. After the demon Mura is killed, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, defeats Jarāsandha's army, and later marries Rukmini, the princess of Vidarbha, by abducting her in the rākṣasa style after defeating her brother Rukmi and other kings like Śālva and Śiśupāla. The chapter also includes the story of King Raivata offering his daughter Revatī to Balarāma, and the sage Nara-Nārāyaṇa's penance.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmini and the Defeat of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Mura, Krishna returns to Dvārakā, defeats Jarāsandha, and then marries Rukmiṇī. The marriage occurs before the great war of Kurukṣetra.
story.comic_potential: High: The dramatic escape from the burning mountain, the abduction of Rukmiṇī amidst a gathering of kings, and the humorous flight of Krishna and Balarāma from Jarāsandha.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a king without contentment suffers, while a contented person sleeps peacefully.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes santosha (contentment) as a key practice for inner peace. This verse directly supports that teaching.

teachings.teaching: The wise bow to brahmins who are content with their lot, compassionate, egoless, and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights humility and service to the wise as part of kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects and ensures their happiness is dear to the sage.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the kriya-yoga principle of righteous action (dharma) in leadership.

teachings.teaching: The Lord, though appearing human, is the supreme being; his actions are lilā (divine play).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary often stresses seeing the divine in all actions, recognizing the play of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna leads to liberation; hearing his glories removes all distress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga includes śravaṇa (hearing) as a key practice for purification.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the only suitable husband for a virtuous woman; no other can compare.
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes the soul's exclusive devotion to the divine, a core theme in bhakti-yoga.

teachings.teaching: One should surrender completely to the Lord, offering oneself as a spouse.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes ātma-nivedana (self-surrender) as a limb of bhakti.

teachings.teaching: The Lord can be attained through rituals, vows, and worship, but ultimately by his grace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates karma-yoga (rituals) with bhakti, but stresses grace as final.

teachings.teaching: The Lord rescues his devotee by force, defeating all obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of divine intervention in kriya-yoga when the devotee is helpless.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the Lord's lotus feet is sought even by great beings like Śiva for purification.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's teaching: the ultimate goal is to attain the dust of the Lord's feet, symbolizing complete surrender.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings gather to prevent Krishna. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and the brahmana returns to reassure her. Krishna and Balarama arrive and are honored by the king. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then leaves the temple, ready to be taken by Krishna.

story.title: Rukmini's Abduction: The Prelude
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs just before the abduction of Rukmini by Krishna.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Rukmini's anxiety, the arrival of enemy kings, the secret plan, and the temple scene.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's response to Rukmini's love shows that divine love is reciprocal and attentive.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sadhana' where the devotee's longing (Rukmini) attracts the divine response (Krishna). The sleeplessness of both indicates the intensity of spiritual yearning.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's prayer to Ambika (Parvati) for Krishna as her husband exemplifies surrender to the divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'Ishvara-pranidhana' (surrender to God). Rukmini's prayer is a model of offering one's desires to the divine, trusting in the outcome.

teachings.teaching: The brahmana messenger's role highlights the importance of a guru or guide in spiritual matters.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru is essential for transmitting divine grace. The brahmana acts as a conduit between Krishna and Rukmini, symbolizing the guru's role in connecting the soul to God.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels, walks gracefully to the temple, captivating the kings. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her before the assembled kings, including Jarāsandha, who are humiliated.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's letter to Krishna, she goes to the temple and Krishna abducts her.
story.comic_potential: Rukmiṇī's graceful walk and the kings' infatuation and fall provide visual comedy.

chapter_summary: After Krishna abducts Rukmini, the Yadava army defeats the assembled kings. Rukmi pursues Krishna, is defeated and humiliated (head shaved) but spared at Rukmini's plea. Balarama then teaches Rukmini about the nature of the self and the illusion of duality.

story.title: The Defeat of Rukmi and Balarama's Teaching to Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation (head shaved) has comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is one in all beings; duality is due to ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes realizing this unity through disciplined practice, transcending the illusion of separate selves.

teachings.teaching: The body is temporary and subject to change; the self is eternal and unchanging.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this knowledge helps detach from bodily identification, a key step in meditation.

teachings.teaching: Sorrow arises from ignorance; it can be removed by knowledge of the truth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga practice dispels ignorance, leading to inner peace.

chapter_summary: After Rukmiṇī's abduction, Śiśupāla, humiliated and enraged, builds the city of Bhojakaṭa and vows not to enter Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. Meanwhile, Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā with great celebration, and the news astonishes neighboring kings.

story.title: Śiśupāla's Vow and Kṛṣṇa's Marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.arc: After the abduction, Śiśupāla's humiliation leads him to build a new city and vow revenge, while Kṛṣṇa marries Rukmiṇī in Dvārakā with grand celebrations.
story.timeline_note: Immediately following Rukmiṇī's abduction from Kuṇḍina.
story.comic_potential: Śiśupāla's dramatic vow and the contrast with the joyous wedding festivities.

teachings.teaching: Letting go of mental agitation and fixing the mind on intelligence (vaimanasyaṃ parityajya mano buddhyā samādadhe).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this is the practice of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and dharana (concentration), calming the mind to focus on higher awareness.

teachings.teaching: The futility of revenge and anger (ahatvā durmatiṃ kṛṣṇam... kuṇḍinaṃ na pravekṣyāmīty uktvā tatrāvasad ruṣā).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as an example of how unresolved anger binds one to suffering, emphasizing the need for inner purification through kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: Celebration of divine union (Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Rukmiṇī) as a symbol of the soul's union with the Divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework, this represents the culmination of bhakti-yoga, where the individual self (jiva) merges with the Supreme Self (Kṛṣṇa) in loving devotion.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. He is stolen as an infant by the demon Shambara, thrown into the ocean, swallowed by a fish, and eventually recovered. Raised by Mayavati (Rati in disguise), he learns his true identity, defeats and kills Shambara, and returns to Dvaraka, reuniting with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, survival, and victorious return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the later pastimes in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High; includes a baby thrown into the sea, swallowed by a fish, raised by a demoness, and a dramatic battle with magical weapons.

teachings.teaching: The divine play of the Lord includes apparent loss and recovery, demonstrating that nothing is ever truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyahara: the withdrawal of the senses from external objects, only to be reunited with the divine source. Pradyumna's journey symbolizes the soul's temporary separation from God and its inevitable return through divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the 'Maha-maya' vidya given to Pradyumna represents the Kriya-Yoga technique that destroys all delusions. The battle with Shambara's magical weapons symbolizes the inner struggle against the mind's illusions.

teachings.teaching: Recognition of the divine through external form and qualities.
teachings.lens_note: Rukmini's recognition of Pradyumna by his resemblance to Krishna reflects the Kriya-Yoga teaching of seeing the same divine consciousness in all beings. The mother's intuitive love transcends intellectual doubt.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about Satrajit's offense to Krishna and the origin of the Syamantaka gem. Shuka narrates: Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, received the Syamantaka gem from Surya. Wearing it, he shone like the sun, causing people to mistake him for Surya. Krishna asked for the gem for King Ugrasena, but Satrajit refused. Later, Satrajit's brother Prasena went hunting wearing the gem, was killed by a lion, which was then killed by Jambavan. When Prasena didn't return, rumors spread that Krishna had killed him for the gem. To clear his name, Krishna tracked the path, found the bodies, entered Jambavan's cave, fought Jambavan for 28 days, and was recognized as the Supreme Lord. Jambavan offered his daughter Jambavati and the gem. Krishna returned to Dwaraka, gave the gem back to Satrajit, who repented and offered his daughter Satyabhama and the gem. Krishna married Satyabhama but declined the gem, saying it should remain with Satrajit as a devotee of the Sun.

story.title: The Syamantaka Gem and the Marriage of Satyabhama
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dwaraka: clearing false accusations, displaying divine strength, and marrying Satyabhama.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's establishment in Dwaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Satrajit as Surya, the rumor mill blaming Krishna, and the 28-day cave fight with Jambavan offer dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord clears His own reputation when falsely accused, showing that He protects His devotees' honor.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'svadharma' – acting righteously to remove obstacles (kleshas) on the path. Krishna's action models how a yogi should actively purify misunderstandings rather than passively accept slander.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth (the gem) can cause envy and conflict; detachment is key.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'vairagya' (dispassion). The gem's troubles show how attachment to objects leads to suffering. Krishna's refusal to keep the gem exemplifies non-attachment.

teachings.teaching: True strength is recognizing the Supreme Lord; Jambavan's surrender after battle shows humility.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with 'Ishvara pranidhana' (surrender to God). Jambavan's realization after defeat is a model for the ego's surrender in yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Kuru fire, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Satadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Satyabhama laments. Krishna and Balarama pursue Satadhanva, who hides the jewel with Akrura. Krishna kills Satadhanva but cannot find the jewel. Balarama goes to Mithila, where he teaches Duryodhana mace-fighting. Krishna returns to Dvaraka, performs funeral rites, and later diplomatically retrieves the jewel from Akrura, clearing his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel: Murder, Pursuit, and Redemption
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Kuru fire incident, before the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: High: murder mystery, chase scene, jewel theft, divine intervention, and a teaching moment.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though omniscient, acts within human conventions to clear his name and restore harmony.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'lila' (divine play) where the absolute engages in relative actions to teach dharma through example.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's devotion and honesty are rewarded; he returns the jewel willingly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes 'sattvic' action: acting without attachment, as Akrura does not cling to the jewel but gives it up when asked.

teachings.teaching: The Syamantaka jewel symbolizes material wealth; its pursuit leads to murder and strife, but when surrendered to Krishna, it brings peace.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that material desires (kama) cause bondage; offering them to the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana) purifies the mind.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (Yamuna's daughter), and later marries Mitravinda and Satyabhama (Nagnajit's daughter) after subduing seven bulls.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Subduing of the Bulls
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satyabhama
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the burning of Khandava forest and before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna splitting into seven forms to subdue the bulls is a visually striking scene.

teachings.teaching: Krishna as the supreme lord who removes the suffering of his devotees (verse 10).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the guru's role in removing obstacles through inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The importance of surrendering to Krishna as the only refuge (verse 21).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes surrender (prapatti) as the key to Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's lila (pastimes) are performed effortlessly, like a child playing (verse 45-46).
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the concept of sahaja (spontaneous) action in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries many wives, including Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Shrutakirti), Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king), and thousands of others rescued after killing the demon Bhauma. Arjuna assists in protecting the wedding party.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages to Satya, Bhadra, Lakshmana, and Others
story.arc: Krishna's marriages and rescue of captive princesses
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Bhauma and rescue of the princesses
story.comic_potential: Arjuna's lion-like routing of attackers and Krishna's multiple marriages

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate his role as the supreme enjoyer and protector of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'bhoga' (enjoyment) as a divine attribute, where Krishna's actions are not for personal pleasure but to liberate souls and establish dharma.

teachings.teaching: Arjuna's protection of Krishna's wedding party shows the importance of serving the divine through one's skills.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes 'kriya' (action) as a path to union with the divine; Arjuna's martial prowess is offered in service to Krishna, exemplifying karma-yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna, accompanied by Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to kill the demon Naraka (Bhauma). He destroys the fortifications, defeats the five-headed demon Mura and his seven sons, and finally beheads Naraka. The Earth goddess (Bhumi) offers Naraka's earrings and other treasures to Krishna, who then rescues 16,000 captive princesses, marries them, and returns to Dvaraka. He also restores Indra's earrings and uproots the Parijata tree for Satyabhama.

relationships.dst: Tāmra, Antarikṣa, Śravaṇa, Vibhāvasu, Vasu, Nabhasvān, Aruṇa

story.title: The Slaying of Naraka and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his power and compassion.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the defeat of Jarasandha and before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: High: The battle scenes with Mura and his sons, the beheading of Naraka, and the humorous uprooting of the Parijata tree.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's power is absolute; he effortlessly destroys demons and their fortifications.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the practitioner's ability to overcome internal obstacles (demons) through disciplined practice and divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The Earth goddess Bhumi's prayer emphasizes that all creation is a manifestation of the one supreme Lord, and duality is an illusion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that realizing this non-duality is the goal of meditation; the prayer is a model for surrendering the ego.

teachings.teaching: Krishna marries the 16,000 princesses, showing that the Lord accepts all who surrender to him, regardless of their past.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of universal acceptance and the transformation of desire into devotion.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pointing out his poverty and lack of royal status, but Rukmini responds with a passionate defense of her devotion, arguing that true wealth is spiritual. Krishna then reveals his teasing was a test, praises her devotion, and assures her of his eternal love.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing and Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's teasing and Rukmini's dramatic reaction, followed by reconciliation, have elements of romantic comedy.

teachings.teaching: True wealth is spiritual devotion, not material possessions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes inner renunciation; Krishna's 'poverty' symbolizes detachment from material opulence.

teachings.teaching: A devotee should not be swayed by external appearances or social status.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner focuses on the inner self (Atman) beyond social labels.

teachings.teaching: The highest goal is love for God, not worldly gains.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga aims at union with the Divine, not material rewards.

teachings.teaching: A true devotee sees the body as temporary and focuses on the lotus feet of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga involves transcending bodily identification through meditation.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her love and grievances to Krishna, recalling how he abducted her at her svayamvara, killed her brother Rukmi, and now seems indifferent. Krishna responds playfully, and the chapter ends with their intimate conversation, illustrating divine pastimes in human form.

story.title: Rukmini's Lament and Krishna's Playful Response
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a householder
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their early married life.
story.comic_potential: High - Rukmini's playful scolding and Krishna's teasing create a light-hearted, intimate scene.

teachings.teaching: Even the Supreme Lord engages in loving reciprocation with his devotees, demonstrating that divine love transcends formalities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sahaja' (natural, spontaneous) devotion where the soul's longing for the Divine is expressed without pretense, mirroring the practitioner's inner dialogue with the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions, though seemingly human, are perfect and meant to teach the soul about surrender and love.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Krishna's 'human' flaws are deliberate plays (lila) to guide the practitioner beyond judgment, into pure bhakti where all actions are seen as divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens and describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana, daughter of Rukmi, and the subsequent dice game where Balarama kills Rukmi after being insulted.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game at Bhojakata
story.arc: Krishna's family life and the conflict with Rukmi
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and other queens, their sons are enumerated. Then the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana occurs, followed by the dice game and Rukmi's death.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's violent reaction to the insult provide dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The queens of Krishna, despite being sixteen thousand, could not conquer his mind with their charms, showing his supreme control.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of a yogi who remains unaffected by sensory distractions, maintaining inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Even Brahma and others do not know the true position of Krishna, indicating his transcendence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the ultimate reality is beyond intellectual comprehension and can only be realized through direct yogic experience.

teachings.teaching: The queens served Krishna with devotion, performing various services.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of selfless service (seva) as a means to purify the mind and attain union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Yogis see past, present, and future, even things distant or hidden.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this refers to the siddhi (perfection) of trikala-jnana (knowledge of three times) attained through advanced practice.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates Bana's boon from Shiva, his arrogance, Usha's dream of Aniruddha, Citralekha's abduction of Aniruddha, and Bana's capture of Aniruddha.

story.title: The Abduction of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Parikshit asks Shuka to narrate the story of Aniruddha's marriage to Usha and the battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka recounts Bana's boon, his arrogance, Usha's dream, Citralekha's abduction of Aniruddha, and Bana's capture of Aniruddha.
story.timeline_note: This story occurs after the Vamana incarnation and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: Usha's dream, Citralekha's magical abduction, Aniruddha's fight with Bana's army, and the impending battle between Krishna and Shiva offer dramatic and visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: Bana's arrogance leads to his downfall; even a boon from Shiva cannot protect one who is proud.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how ego (asmita) binds the soul; Bana's pride in his thousand arms is a metaphor for attachment to material power.

teachings.teaching: Usha's love for Aniruddha is predestined; divine will brings them together.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the soul's innate longing for the divine (Aniruddha represents the unhindered Self). The dream symbolizes the inner call of the Atman.

teachings.teaching: Citralekha's yogic power enables her to transport Aniruddha; yoga transcends physical limitations.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle that through disciplined practice, one can attain siddhis (perfections) and overcome spatial constraints.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, with various warriors clashing. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's arms except four, and spares him due to Prahlada's boon. Bana surrenders and returns Aniruddha and Usha.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's captivity for four months; part of the Bana-yuddha episode.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, magical weapons, and a fever personification; humorous moment with Kotara appearing naked.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the source of all, beyond material categories, and is realized by the pure-hearted.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as pure consciousness, beyond the modifications of the mind.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet, having obtained a human birth, is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the rarity of human birth and the importance of using it for self-realization through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is available to all, even to demons, when they surrender.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of surrender (prapatti) as a means to transcend karma.

chapter_summary: After being blessed by Lord Shiva, Lord Krishna returns to his capital Dvaraka with his wives. The city is beautifully decorated, and he enters amidst the sounds of conches and drums, welcomed by citizens, friends, and brahmanas. The chapter concludes by stating that one who recites this account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's Return to Dvaraka After Defeating Shiva
story.arc: Krishna's victory over Shiva and return to his capital
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Shiva, Krishna returns to Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: A grand procession with decorated streets, conches, and drums, as the victorious hero returns home with his family.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the account of Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one never faces defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of remembrance (smarana) as a practice to align with divine will and overcome obstacles. The morning recitation purifies the mind and sets a victorious tone for the day.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his story to Kṛṣṇa. He had donated millions of cows to brāhmaṇas, but due to a mix-up where one cow was given to a different brāhmaṇa, he was cursed to become a lizard. After being touched by Kṛṣṇa, he regains his divine form and ascends to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the Yadus about the severe consequences of misappropriating brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: High: The dramatic rescue of a giant lizard from a well, the revelation of a king's past, and the moral lesson about brāhmaṇa property.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriation of brāhmaṇa property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the principle of karma and the importance of purity in action (kriya). The story illustrates how even unintentional misdeeds bind one to suffering, emphasizing the need for vigilant awareness in all actions.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a brāhmaṇa, even if he is at fault; instead, one should bow to him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a call to cultivate humility and respect for the spiritual class, which represents the inner divine principle. It aligns with the practice of surrendering the ego (ahamkāra) to the higher self.

teachings.teaching: The touch of a realized soul (like Kṛṣṇa) can instantly liberate one from the results of past actions.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the power of divine grace (kṛpā) and the guru's touch to accelerate spiritual evolution and dissolve karmic bonds.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Vrindavan, meets the gopas and gopis, consoles them with Krishna's messages, spends two months there, enjoys with the gopis, drinks varuni, and in a drunken state drags the Yamuna river with his plow when she disobeys his call. The river submits and he releases her, then bathes and adorns himself.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vrindavan and the Dragging of the Yamuna
story.arc: Balarama returns to Vrindavan, consoles the gopis, enjoys with them, and displays his divine power by disciplining the Yamuna river.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Balarama visits Vrindavan during the spring season (Madhu and Madhava months).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken antics, the gopis' emotional outbursts, and the river's personified fear create a mix of humor and drama.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna shows the pinnacle of devotion, where they gave up everything for him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the complete surrender of the ego (ahamkara) to the divine, a key step in the path of devotion.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent mercy toward Yamuna illustrates the Lord's authority over nature and his compassion for the repentant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the disciplining of the mind (Yamuna as the river of thoughts) by the divine will, leading to purification.

chapter_summary: Pauṇḍraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vāsudeva and sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa demanding he surrender. Kṛṣṇa laughs, kills Pauṇḍraka and his ally the king of Kāśī. The king of Kāśī's son Sudakṣiṇa performs a black magic ritual to summon a fiery demon, but Kṛṣṇa's Sudarśana cakra destroys the demon and burns Vārāṇasī.

story.title: The Killing of Pauṇḍraka and the Burning of Vārāṇasī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the Supreme Lord, demonstrating his supremacy over false claimants and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's return to Dvārakā from Vraja.
story.comic_potential: High – the absurdity of Pauṇḍraka's imitation, the fiery demon, and the cakra's destruction provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: False claims to divinity lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches that egoistic identification with the Self (aham brahmasmi) without realization is dangerous; one must purify the mind before claiming unity.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the inner Self (Kṛṣṇa) automatically dissolves obstacles when one is established in meditation.

teachings.teaching: Black magic (abhicāra) recoils on the user when directed against the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the law of karma: actions performed with selfish intent boomerang; in Kriya-Yoga, it warns against using spiritual powers for harm.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a monkey friend of Narakāsura, who wreaks havoc across the land to avenge his friend. He disrupts sacrifices, imprisons people, and finally confronts Balarāma on Raivataka mountain. After a fierce battle, Balarāma kills Dvivida with his fist, and is praised by the celestials.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Narakāsura's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's lewd gestures and Balarāma's drunken state add humor.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the righteous and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma: actions have consequences, and divine intervention restores cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful demon cannot withstand the Lord's might.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that ego and pride lead to downfall; surrender to the divine is the path to liberation.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Jambavati and Krishna, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura as a peace envoy but is insulted. Enraged, he uproots the city with his plow and threatens to throw it into the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts, and Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the svayamvara of Lakshmana, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's rage and the city being uprooted like a toy is visually dramatic and humorous.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's anger is for the sake of instructing the world, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine actions are always pedagogical, aimed at guiding souls toward dharma. The apparent anger is a tool to correct and teach, not a personal emotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole support and cause of creation, maintenance, and dissolution.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the ultimate reality (Brahman) is the substratum of all. This verse affirms that the Lord alone is the cause, and all beings are his playthings.

teachings.teaching: Humility and surrender are the proper responses to the Lord's power.
teachings.lens_note: The Kauravas' surrender exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of prapatti (self-surrender) as the means to attain grace and liberation.

chapter_summary: Balarama returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage, recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly, and the city still bears marks of his prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return and Narration
story.arc: Balarama completes his pilgrimage and returns to Dvaraka, where he recounts his deeds to the Yadava elders.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the pilgrimage described in previous chapters.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's dramatic recounting of his exploits, with the city still showing signs of his strength.

teachings.teaching: The importance of sharing one's spiritual experiences with the community.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of sanga (association) and the need to integrate individual practice with collective wisdom.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,000 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace, engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and leaves, understanding the divine play.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: Krishna's Yoga-Maya
story.arc: Narada, curious about Krishna's simultaneous presence with 16,000 wives, goes to Dvaraka. He sees Krishna in different palaces engaged in various activities, realizing it is yoga-maya. He praises Krishna and departs.
story.timeline_note: After Narada's previous visit and the marriage of Krishna to many wives.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered reactions as he sees Krishna in multiple places at once, each time being welcomed as if just arrived.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of simultaneous awareness and action in multiple states of consciousness, achievable through advanced yogic practice.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion: he recognizes Krishna's greatness and seeks to understand his divine play.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of a guru's humility and the student's sincere inquiry to comprehend the divine.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's engagement in worldly activities while remaining transcendent shows the ideal of detached action.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's teaching of performing actions without attachment, maintaining inner stillness.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, including his morning activities, worship, and courtly life. A messenger from imprisoned kings appeals for help against Jarāsandha. Nārada arrives and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice, seeking Krishna's approval.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Appeal of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice and liberation of kings from Jarāsandha
story.timeline_note: Set in Dvaraka during Krishna's earthly pastimes, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful reluctance to leave his queens' embrace, the dramatic entrance of Nārada, and the contrast between the serene court and the desperate plea of the kings.

teachings.teaching: One should rise early in the brāhma-muhūrta, meditate on the Supreme Self, and perform daily duties with devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of disciplined morning practice (sādhana) to align with the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is self-luminous, beyond material qualities, and the cause of creation and destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight this as the realization of the non-dual Brahman, the goal of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Charity and respect to brāhmaṇas, cows, and devotees are essential for spiritual purification.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga sees such actions as purifying the mind and creating conducive conditions for inner meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes and names purify even the lowest, and hearing, chanting, and meditating on them lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress the power of nāma-japa and kīrtana as integral to Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Material happiness is dream-like and dependent on others; true happiness is found in the self, free from desire.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching of turning inward to discover the bliss of the Self (ānanda).

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. He travels to Indraprastha, is warmly received by Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas, and stays there for several months.

story.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
